narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds. The model estimated a 94% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is why your application was rejected—admissions prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing to ensure program success rates. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 school grade decile, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school grade decile, all of which pushed the model's assessment toward expecting failure rather than success. Your Year 3 grades placed you in the second decile at your school—well below the typical fifth-decile average—which had the strongest negative pull, signaling to the model a concerning dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles for students in similar positions. Similarly, your LSAT score of 28 was a major drag, far under the average of about 37 seen in the dataset, making it the second-most influential factor in tipping the scales against you. Your Year 1 grades, also in the second decile versus the usual fifth, rounded out the top concerns, reinforcing a pattern of lower academic standing compared to peers who typically pass the bar. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was close to the average of 3.21 and your middle family income bracket aligned with many others, along with planning full-time study like over 90% of students, these didn't outweigh the top issues. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often enter with mid-to-high deciles and LSATs in the mid-30s or better, your profile stood out as higher-risk based on these key metrics. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances succeed with targeted effort—but the model focuses on these predictors to guide decisions. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, as even a few points could shift predictions dramatically, and reflect on strategies to improve your upper-year grades if reapplying. Strengthening these areas through prep courses or academic advising could position you much more competitively next time. You're clearly motivated to pursue law, and with focused improvements on your grades and test performance, future applications could tell a different story. Keep pushing—many overcome early hurdles like this.
